interviewStages: Behavioral: ownership, working with enterprise customers, deep technical work
systemDesignTopics: Design a global CDN with origin shielding, cache invalidation, and hot-object handling
Design DDoS mitigation at the edge that scrubs traffic without hurting legitimate users
Design global anycast/DNS routing that steers users to the nearest healthy edge

coding: Implement a fixed-size LRU cache for edge objects with O(1) get/put; discuss eviction and concurrency at the edge.
Given a stream of requests, implement a sliding-window rate limiter that works across distributed edge nodes.
Parse and validate DNS/HTTP header inputs defensively; handle malformed and hostile input.
Compute shortest paths over a network graph with edge weights (latency). Discuss algorithm choice at scale.
Implement consistent hashing for cache-key distribution across a changing set of edge servers.
behavioral: Akamai's core values include Urgency & Persistence. Tell me about a time you drove a hard problem to completion when others gave up.
'Customer First' and 'One Akamai.' Describe a time you worked across teams to solve an enterprise customer's problem.
Integrity & Trust is a stated value. Tell me about a time you did the right thing even when it was costly.
Describe a deep systems problem you owned end-to-end. What made it hard?
Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate on a networking or architecture decision.
systemDesign: Design a global CDN with origin shielding, cache invalidation, and hot-object handling.
Design DDoS mitigation at the edge that scrubs traffic without hurting legitimate users.
Design a global anycast/DNS routing strategy for steering users to the nearest healthy edge.
values: Akamai runs quietly critical internet infrastructure. What draws you to unglamorous but essential systems work?
How do you think about reliability and trust when your customers are enterprises depending on you?
Giving Back is an Akamai value. Tell me about a time you invested in your community or teammates beyond your role.
Why Akamai over a newer, more fashionable edge/CDN company?
other: How does Akamai compare to Cloudflare or Fastly?

⏱ 1 min read

Akamai
akamai.com ↗

Akamai is the original CDN — running the largest distributed edge network on the internet for 25+ years. Now expanding into edge compute (after acquiring Linode) and security. The interview is networking-heavy and rewards engineers with serious systems chops.

Process

Recruiter screen → 60-minute technical phone (DSA + networking trivia) → onsite virtual: 2 coding, 1 system design, 1 craft deep-dive, 1 behavioral. Cycle: 4–6 weeks.

What they actually ask

- Design a global CDN with origin shielding and cache invalidation

- Design BGP routing strategies for global edge presence

- Design DDoS mitigation at the edge

- Coding: medium-hard DSA, often with networking flavor

- Behavioral: ownership, working with enterprise customers, deep technical work

Levels and comp (2026)

- SE II: $140K–$170K total

- Senior SE: $200K–$260K

- Staff: $280K–$370K

- Principal: $400K–$520K

Akamai comp lags newer cloud companies; benefits and stability are strong.

Prep priorities

- Be fluent in C and at least one modern language (Go, Rust)

- Understand networking deep: TCP, BGP, DNS, anycast

- Read about Akamai's engineering history and recent edge compute moves

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Akamai remote-friendly?

Hybrid in Cambridge MA, Bangalore, Krakow. Some engineering roles remote within US.

How does Akamai compare to Cloudflare or Fastly?

Akamai is the largest by network footprint; Cloudflare wins on developer experience; Fastly wins on edge programmability. Akamai pays below both on compensation.

Is the engineering culture stale?

Mature and engineering-rigorous. Less fashionable than newer companies but solid technical work for those who care about real networking problems.
